speaker: interviewer
text: I'm speaking to Cissie Nathan-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting this cough. I think I'm getting  this cough. Excuse me. [COUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] I'm speaking to Cissie Nathan, and this is tape five. You were beginning to tell me how you met your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was invited to a dinner party, and he was there. That's all. Never spoke to him. [LAUGHS] I said to my brother, I think he's got Saint Vitus Dance. He can't sit still. [LAUGHS] That was just a question. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working, naturally. And used to go to London to the West End every day. But shame enough, on Saturday morning, I had to go and get some shopping. Because I used to cook on Sunday-- fry a bit of fish and cook for the weekend, so that when I came home every evening, I would have food.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time my son-- he'll tell you his story-- was traveling. So I used to say-- he was on a ship. He'll tell you his story. And, uh, I used-- he used to write to me, and I used to write to him that there was a letter at every port of call. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where he stopped. So that's what happened. Uh-- Then once, many weeks after-- and I never used to get visited, because my brother and my aunt-- both my brothers were hair dressers. My eldest brother had a salon of his own. He and his wife worked there. And I never used to see them. Because they were-- and my sister Anne was also working in a millinery. And she was dealing with my youngest brother then, with her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh, I never saw anybody weekends. So I used to cook for the week. Then one Sunday morning-- I was frying fish as it happened-- there was a ring at the bell. And I thought, well, who can that be? So I walked to the door, and there was this gentleman I'd met at the dinner. So I didn't say hello, I said, how did you get my address? [LAUGHS] It was the first thing I said. So he said, I phoned your brother. He said, I was in the area, so I thought I'd knock at the door. He used to live in Hove in Sussex and traveled back and forth to go to the business. But never on a Shabbat. He used to come home early on Friday. So, um, what could I do? I had to ask him in. So he said that he used to go to the business Sunday morning. So I said, uh-- he told me he'd been to the business. So I said, where are you going now? He said, I'm going back to Hove. So I said, would you like cuppa tea? So he said, yeah. So I gave him a cup of tea with a piece of cake or something. I don't remember. And we sat there talking. And then he said, well I have to go because I have to get-- get to Victoria to get the train. So he said, before I go, can I ask you something? I said, yeah. Would you mind giving me a piece of fish to take back with me? [LAUGHS] So I gave him a couple of pieces of fish. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I didn't hear for a while. And then he came again. And he said he'd been invited to a wedding and he'd like me to come with him as a companion. I wasn't ready for that. So I said, I don't really. He said, please do me a favor. There's nobody else that I would like to ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother, the youngest one who was a hairdresser, I got in touch with him. He came-- he said, I'll come and do your hair on this Sunday that the wedding was. Then [LAUGHS] he came one day, and he said to me, I'm sorry, I can't take you. Because the family say it's not right. So I didn't go. I had bought a dress and shoes special. I didn't have anything like that. So when my hairdresser brother came, he said, come on. I'll do your hair. I said no, I'm not going. [LAUGHS] So that was the end of that. But then he-- then my oldest brother, one Sunday-- they'd got a car. But I never saw him on a Sunday. Never took me anywhere. He lived near me. So not far. So he said, sis, would you like to come for a ride next Sunday in the car? So I said, oh, that's a surprise. So he said, well, we thought for a change we'd take you for a ride. So I said, all right. Thank you very much. What time will you fetch? So he fetched me. And he said-- on the way, I never asked-- on the way I said, by the way, where are we going? He said, we're going to Brighton. So I said, do  not-- oh, I don't know. I said, I don't like Brighton. He said, well, I've made arrangements to-- to go to Brighton, because we're going to have lunch in a hotel. So I said, all right. So I went. We're going to Brighton. I'd been there before, you see, and it's all stones. I said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to the hotel. We had lunch. And then he said, well, to be honest, we've been invited to a-- people I know for tea. So I said, oh, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go with him. Because it was a different area. It was Hove. We just-- continuation. So I went with him and went to this place. It was in the Drive Hove. Went to this flat. When I got there, he was there. And his youngest married sister was there, without her husband. Her name was Sophie. She was a sweet person. And of course you can imagine how I felt, but I didn't say anything. His sister whispered to my sister-in-law, and I heard it, this is the first time in his life he's ever bought flowers. So you know, you sit and think. Anyway. We had tea and I said goodbye, and that was it. I never thought about it. So I said to my brother, if I'd have known, I wouldn't have come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he give you the flowers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were in a vase. In a vase. The vase is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how much later did you marry? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sometime later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't know exactly how long. Month or two he got in touch with me. He came and, uh, he asked me to marry him. So I said, I must get in touch with my son. I have a son. I said, I've told you before. I have in touch with my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got in touch to Mauri-- with Maurice by letter, air mail. And I told him. And he wrote me a letter back and said, Mother, I'd be much more contented and not worry so much if I knew that you were settled. So I thought, all right. I'll do it. I can't say that I was in love with him, like my first husband. I cannot say that. He was a nice man in his own way. But strictly orthodox. Strict. So we got married in Brighton Shore on-- in December, the end of December. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, 1950. Yeah. Yeah. And then I kept-- we kept the place on in London. Because it was nice if we were visiting-- visiting relatives, we could stay there overnight, see. And then, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in a flat in Hove. And, uh, Maurice was still on the ships. And then he decided we better get a house. Because of-- so we bought a house in Hove, in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a wholesale woollen merchant. He used to go to the mills up in north of London-- uh, north of England, and buy and sell to Harrods, to Harrington's, a big store in Hove, and different houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died here in Palm Springs. We were here on a holiday to visit children. He died in Palm Springs in 19-- I think it's 12 years ago now. 1982, I think, he died. Yeah. He's buried here in Palm Springs. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back to the house in England, yes. And I lived there, because my son was here then. I lived there for a while by myself. And then I had to move-- I mo-- he left me the house. It was on both our name, but he left me the house. And, uh, one night I'd get-- I used to get terrible phone calls in the middle of the night. So I was scared. And, uh, we had a special laundry man, because we didn't have these laundry machines then. And he used to come every Monday morning. And he said to me one morning-- Wilson's Laundry, it was. And, uh, one of the men came to pick it up and bring it back. So he said to me, Mrs. Nathan, whatever's wrong with you, you look terrible. And I told him. And he said, you must get onto the police and the telephone people. Which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the police said they couldn't do anything to help me. But I did see a policeman now and again walk up and down the road, you see. But, uh, the telephone put-- put-- telephone people put my phone on that intermission, interjection. And if somebody phoned me, if my brother phoned me from Bournemouth, they would ask me first, do you want to speak to this person? If I said yes, he-- they put me through. Otherwise they wouldn't. And when did you finally come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my son brought me here in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think a year or so-- no. Then, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He decided he would-- he was married in England to Terry. And we decided to, uh, go and live in Bournemouth. First I lived in Bournemouth in a flat. Then they decided-- they were here-- to come and live in Bournemouth. So then bought a big house in Bournemouth. And then we lived there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: After he got married, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: After he married. Yes, of course. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we sold the house, because decided that they were coming here. So we were-- he had a house here, and that's what happened. And my-- that's how I came in 1980 with my husband and the-- couple of times stayed with them before he died, of course. And that's how he died here at that time. And now I'm with them all the time. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a-- a very-- very good son and daughter-in-law. They look after me better than I look after my-- looked after myself. I can truly say that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son will tell you all about that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was married before, and divorced. But he will tell you about that himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? I was 90 on the 8th of November. That's why all these cards and flowers. Yeah. And I've had presents and telegrams and faxes from all over, from as far as Australia. My family and relatives in Australia always keep in touch, always send photographs. They would love me-- they've had bar mitzvahs. For-- one-- one-- my cousin has two daughters married to South Africans. They lived in South Africa at that time. And one has three sons. And the other one has two sons and a daughter. So every bar mitzvah they've asked me to come. And I've never gone. And I'm too old to travel on my own now. The last bar mitzvah was in September. And I have all the pictures and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You gathered a lot of wisdom during these years. Would you like to share some of this wisdom with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I know is, and I don't always do it, a still tongue keeps a still mind. But I don't always do it. Sometimes I say things. Afterwards I think, I shouldn't say it. But sometimes you have to. You know, you-- you get the urge to say it. But otherwise, no. I have a lot of friends. I have a friend in Hove I've known since 1950. She's a few years younger than me. I have lot-- I have some relatives still in Holland, in the family. My late father-in-law's family, yeah. And some of my mum's, mother's-- my grandmother's, really, children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have seen a lot of tragedy. You lived through a lot of tragedy. What would you like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid for them. I'm truly afraid for them, of what's going on in Israel today. Because Arafat won't stop. He wants the Golan Heights, and he wants Jerusalem. And that's what I'm scared of. That's what I'm really scared of. Palestine was Jordan. They gave Pales-- they gave Palestine, where the Palestinians used to live, to Jordan's father after the war, the first war. So they should put them back there and leave Israel as he was. My second husband fought in the Jewish Battalion in England and fought in Palestine when the British were there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's a book, there's a museum in Netanya, in Israel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that have a book of-- that-- In 1960 we went to Israel, because he heard there was a museum-- no. We went to Israel with a group from the shul. And somebody told him there was a museum there. So we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went first with a-- a neighbor of mine who was in the group and came back. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they said they were gonna-- going to make a, like a room where people could come and sit and see a film of that time. Did he have any pictures of that time? And he said, yes. So we sent them to the museum. And, uh, the ambassador in-- the Israeli ambassador in London sent-- sent him a pin and a letter. And it's like a small Jewish menorah, which I have somewhere. I don't remember at the moment where.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he died here. And my nephew-- who was in the camp-- daughter went to school in Israel for a year. So I went with him and my sister, God rest her soul, to see her. And then we went to the museum. And they asked me if there were any more pictures. So I said, well, I don't know, but I will go through his cabinet, which I was never allowed to do. [LAUGHS] Go through his cabinet and see. And I'd found some more. And I said-- that was before-- And then I went with my nephew and my sister to the-- there. And, um, I saw the pictures on the screen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you say to Jewish children in the diaspora? How can they protect themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what I think?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I'm just-- I said, I always said, if you go to Israel, at least you can fight for your life. Anywhere else, you've got no chance. That's what I think. That's why I've said that. And that's all. Please finish it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very much. For a few minutes, maybe, we'll call your son and your daughter-in-law to join you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Nathan, will you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Of course. What would I have done without him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce him to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Maurice. And this is my daughter-in-law Terry Jane. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're staying with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live with them. In there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, I understand that you were with your mother in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was. Excuse my voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I know you have a problem with your voice today. So we're just going to ask you a few questions. [LAUGHS] Um, do you remember that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went with them to camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, when we went to the first camp, I was 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything that happened before the time that we went to the camp. And most of what happened during the-- both camps that I was in personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you separated from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of 1944 in Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there when your sister passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- what do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, after my father went away with the other men, and the next day, my mother went away with the other women, we children, about 60 of us, who became known, as it became known, as the Diamond Transport. We stayed behind in Belsen, the children, without our parents. And we were put into a barrack separate from the barrack and the part of the camp where we had been before. We didn't know where our parents had gone. We just remained in that barrack, the children. Until all of a sudden there appeared a young Polish woman. This Polish woman looked after us in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was Jewish. But she didn't speak any Dutch, which is really my own language. But she looked after us, uh, with the consent, obviously, of the Germans. We stayed, all the children stayed together from the end of 1944, December 1944, until we were liberated on the 15th of April, 1945. This woman became known after the war as the Angel of Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Her name is Luba Tryszynska. That woman, after being decorated in Holland by the government of Holland for looking after the Dutch children, known as the Children of the Diamond Transport in Belsen, she left for the United States. And it wasn't until February of this year, 1995, that I had a phone call, here, in [INAUDIBLE] to ask me if I was Maurice Rabbie, which is my own birth name. I told this man, who had a broken accent, it depends who was asking the question who I was. And then he spoke to me in Dutch. And I recognized him, obviously, that he was Dutch, as I am originally. And he revealed to me-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that he, together with a few other children survivors of that transport, had located Luba living in Florida, north of Miami. It was quite amazing, as obviously, this year, 1995 is 50 years since the end of World War II. In March-- in April, sorry. In April this year, he, that man, by that time had contacted about 26 children of that transport. And we all went, these 26 out of the original 60, we all went from different parts of the world to Amsterdam to be reunited, A, for the time in our own city, B, with Luba, who came from Florida. And as it turned out, um, Luba was presented by another medal on behalf of the Queen of Holland by the Lord Mayor of Amsterdam. It was the highest honor that Holland could bestow on someone that is not Dutch by birth. We had a few days, all of us together, in Amsterdam. And one of the days, which happened to work out as the second day of seder, ni-- seder, 1995, was on the 15th of April this year, which is exactly 50 years to the day that we were all liberated in Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You changed your name, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I changed my name in 1953 when I lived in England to Raynor, R-A-Y-N-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Raynor, when you married Maurice, did you know that he was a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Embarrassed to admit, absolutely nothing, until I met him. So it's been very enlightening, needless to say, all these years. We've been married just 10. Actually, in April this year was 10 years, and 15 together. And it's been an honor. And just words sort of leave me, speechless is really what it has been, knowing both of them, and the family, of what is left in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you think that there is really a difference, a real difference between a person that lived through the Holocaust and a person that was born in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the difference is?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are so very many. We're very--

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --very removed here in this country, being isolated from Europe and all of its horrors that the people have seen in their countries that we here, luckily enough, do not experience. And it gives-- it gives very special [SIGHS] um, insight to life, that people here just really don't have. And I'm including my-- including myself in that. Although, now more than I had prior to meeting them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think of your mother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remarkable. Very remarkable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she is very lucky to have both of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wish to thank you for joining us and talking to us. I understand you two have the same birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's fate. I thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
